title: Jilly and Mia Workbench Console
summary: A long rolling console styled after an industrial workbench, with notched 2x4 legs threaded onto a through-stretcher, a slatted 1x6 lower shelf, and a 2x10 plank top.
description:
The signature joint here is the notched leg: each of the eight leg pieces gets a 3/4"-deep notch cut with repeated circular-saw passes and cleaned out with a chisel, so the middle stretcher passes THROUGH the legs rather than butting against them. That's what gives this piece its exposed-joinery workbench look, and it's also what makes the whole base rock-solid once the stretcher is screwed and glued into every notch.

Decorative 3" lag screws with exposed heads reinforce the show-face joints for the look, not the strength — the actual holding power comes from the screws and glue underneath. Add the casters last, after the base is fully assembled and square, since a rolling base is much harder to true up than a stationary one.

tools.note: Cleaning out the notches after the circular saw passes.
Notching the end shelf boards to fit around the legs.

materials.name: 3" lag screws
materials.note: Decorative exposed heads at the notched joints.

cutList.part: Bottom shelf aprons, front and back

steps.title: Notch the leg pieces
steps.body: Notch out all eight leg pieces. Set the circular saw's blade depth to 3/4" and make repeated cuts across the notched area roughly every 1/4", then use a chisel to clean out the waste between the cuts.

steps.title: Assemble the leg sets around the middle stretcher
steps.body: Fit the middle 2x6 stretcher inside each leg set through the notches, and attach with 2-1/2" screws driven from inside (starting in the longer leg piece) and glue. Take care to keep everything square — the shorter bottom stretcher can act as a spacer to help true it up.

steps.title: Attach the bottom stretcher
steps.body: Attach the shorter bottom stretcher with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws. If you don't have a pocket hole jig, screw in at an angle instead, or notch it the same way as the middle stretcher. Drill the pocket holes at 3/4" rather than 1-1/2" so the screw doesn't land in the gap between the two leg boards.

steps.title: Attach the top leg piece
steps.body: Attach the top 2x4 in place with 2-1/2" screws and glue. This completes the two leg sets.

steps.title: Add the top studs
steps.body: Attach the 2x4 top studs between the top aprons — these will support the tabletop in a later step.

steps.title: Attach the center legs
steps.body: Attach the center legs to the top and bottom aprons with 2-1/2" wood screws and glue.

steps.title: Fill the void behind the center leg
steps.body: Fill the void behind the center leg with the smaller inside center leg piece.

steps.title: Add the shelf board supports
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 shelf board supports to the insides of the 2x6 aprons, set about 3/4" down to leave room for the shelf boards.

steps.title: Lay the bottom shelf boards
steps.body: Starting in the center and working outward, lay the 1x6 shelf boards down. The end boards need to be notched with a jigsaw to fit around the legs. Glue and nail down.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop
steps.body: Attach the 2x10 top boards to the studs and aprons with 2-1/2" screws and glue, keeping the overhangs even on the ends and front/back.

steps.title: Attach the casters and finish
steps.body: Attach the caster wheels to the bottom of the legs. Sand and finish as desired.

images.alt: A long rolling console table with notched leg joinery styled after an industrial workbench
